meta.name: forge-kb
meta.description: Shared knowledge base for all Vikash projects — AI FORGE, forge-router, AI-ForgeStream, EBS_Resize, Vikash-Portfolio, ai-router

chunks.heading: Who is Vikash Jaiswal
chunks.body: Vikash Jaiswal is a Lead Platform Engineer and Principal DevOps Architect with 16+ years engineering enterprise-scale cloud and AI platforms. Based in Noida / Gurgaon, India. He designs and operates Kubernetes infrastructure across 19 clusters in 7 global regions, builds multi-LLM AI systems from scratch, and specialises in DevSecOps, RAG architectures, and AI Gateway design. Key projects: AI FORGE (enterprise AI agent OS), forge-router (multi-LLM gateway), forge-SRE (production observability platform — OTel + SLO burn-rate alerting + DORA), AI-ForgeStream (Kubernetes-native media processing pipeline), Production RAG System, EBS Auto-Remediation. Currently Technical Lead at Thales Group, building custom Terraform Providers and GenAI-driven automation tooling. Open to networking with fellow engineers and leaders.

chunks.heading: Key Impact Numbers
chunks.body: 19 Kubernetes clusters managed across 7 global regions. 100+ CI/CD pipelines. 99.99% uptime SLA maintained. 11 domain AI skill packs authored. 10 LLM providers integrated in forge-router. ~60% reduction in engineer troubleshooting ramp-up time from AI FORGE deployment. Zero-secret credential architecture with no credential leakage incidents.

chunks.heading: AI FORGE — Enterprise AI Agent Operating System
chunks.body: AI FORGE (CRESCENT) is an enterprise AI Agent Operating System built on Claude Sonnet via AWS Bedrock. It transforms standard AI assistants into domain-expert platform engineers with persistent cross-session memory, vault-grade security, and modular intelligence. The Crescent Architecture wraps every platform interaction through unified scripts so credentials never enter the AI context. 11 domain skill packs cover Kubernetes, Vault, Jenkins, Jira, MySQL, alerts, security, and query engines. Deployed across 7 global regions managing 19 EKS clusters.

chunks.heading: Crescent Architecture Pattern
chunks.body: The Crescent Architecture is a wrapper-first pattern. Engineers invoke slash commands that map to domain skill packs. Skills invoke unified CLI wrappers with auth injected at runtime from ~/.platform/credentials. Wrappers call platform APIs: Maqui LINQ query engine, MySQL/RDS, Vault/OpenBao, EKS clusters. A Global Brain CLAUDE.md is always loaded. Persistent cross-session memory retains behavioral corrections and incident context. Structural deny-list blocks destructive operations at shell level before they reach the AI layer.
chunks.mermaid:
```mermaid
graph TD
    User[Engineer / AI Agent] -- Slash Commands --> Skills[11 Domain Skills]
    Skills -- Unified Wrappers --> Tools[CLI Wrappers & Aliases]
    Tools -- Auth Injected --> APIs[Platform APIs / Services]
    
    subgraph Core_Services [Platform Services]
        APIs -- Query LINQ --> Malote[Query Engine]
        APIs -- MySQL --> RDS[Installation/Affinity DB]
        APIs -- Vault --> Secrets[Vault/OpenBao]
        APIs -- K8s --> EKS[19+ Clusters]
    end

    subgraph Config_Layer [Local Config]
        Memory[(Persistent Memory)]
        Settings[Settings.json / Permissions]
        Creds[~/.platform/credentials]
    end

    User -.-> Memory
    User -.-> Settings
    User -.-> Creds
```

chunks.heading: AI FORGE RAG Evolution — 7 Phase Roadmap
chunks.body: Phase 1 (complete): File-based primitive RAG — 11 SKILL.md files loaded deterministically, 3200 token baseline. Phase 2: Compact 200-token skill headers, 90% token reduction for exploration. Phase 3: ChromaDB embedded chunk retrieval, 83% token reduction. Phase 4: Qdrant on EKS with hybrid BM25 + dense vector search, corpus expansion to Confluence, Jira, GitLab ADRs. Phase 5: Cross-encoder reranking with ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2. Phase 6: forge-router AI Gateway integration — rate limiting, circuit breaker, PII routing. Phase 7: LLM-as-a-Judge continuous improvement loop with DORA KPIs.

chunks.heading: Zero-Secret Credential Architecture
chunks.body: Credentials are never in AI context, git history, or logs. All platform credentials live in a single ~/.platform/credentials file sourced at runtime by wrapper scripts. The AI agent never sees raw tokens or passwords — it sees only the command output. Structural deny-list blocks rm -rf, reboot, rollout restart, delete, drain at the shell wrapper level before execution. All destructive operations require explicit human confirmation. Read-only defaults across all platform integrations.

chunks.heading: 11 Domain Skill Packs
chunks.body: devo-query: Maqui LINQ and Malote query engine operations. devo-infra: EKS cluster management across 7 regions, Terraform, Helm, Karpenter. devo-tools: DevTool platform wrappers and GitLab integration. devo-database: MySQL/RDS schema management and query optimization. devo-alert: Prometheus/Grafana/Dynatrace alert triage and runbook automation. devo-security: Vault/OpenBao secrets management, IAM policy auditing. devo-devtool: Developer tooling, build system integration. devo-jira: Ticket lifecycle management and sprint automation. automation-offboarding: Employee offboarding credential revocation workflows. automation-resilience-infra: Chaos engineering and resilience testing patterns. automation-tabularasa: Clean-slate environment provisioning.

chunks.heading: forge-router — Multi-LLM Orchestration Engine
chunks.body: forge-router is a production-ready multi-LLM routing engine built in Python. It routes prompts across 10 AI providers using intent-based classification and health-check-first automatic fallback. Providers: Groq (LLaMA 3.3 70B), Antigravity (Gemini Flash), Claude (Anthropic), OpenAI (GPT-4o), Codex, Copilot, Hermes, Gemini, Sakana AI, Ollama (local). Embedded FAISS + SQLite RAG knowledge base with auto-consolidation. Native macOS WKWebView live preview window for rendering mermaid diagrams. Interactive TUI with prompt-toolkit and Rich. Intent-tuned timeouts: chat 15s, code 30s, reasoning 45s, agentic 60s.

chunks.heading: forge-router System Architecture
chunks.body: RouterEngine classifies intent via sub-1ms regex matching (code/reasoning/agentic/summarization/chat). Each intent maps to an ordered provider list. Engine checks health first, attempts the highest-priority available provider, falls back automatically on failure or timeout. RoutingContext preserves multi-turn conversation history across provider switches. FAISS IndexFlatIP (768-dim cosine) stores interaction embeddings from nomic-embed-text via Ollama. Auto-consolidation extracts key facts every 10 interactions via Groq. Per-provider quality scoring via observability module. Two-tier preview lifecycle: HTTP server on :7654 stays bound for the full session, WKWebView window toggled independently.
chunks.mermaid:
```mermaid graph TD User([Developer / AI Agent]) CLI[forge CLI
chat · ask · status · doctor] Chat[ForgeChat REPL
prompt-toolkit · Rich] Router[RouterEngine
intent classify → fallback chain] subgraph Providers [10 LLM Providers] P1[Groq — LLaMA 3.3 70B] P2[Antigravity — Gemini Flash] P3[Claude — Anthropic] P4[OpenAI — GPT-4o] P5[Codex — Code specialist] P6[Hermes — Reasoning] P7[Gemini — Google] P8[Sakana AI] P9[Copilot — GitHub] P10[Ollama — Local / Offline] end subgraph Memory [Knowledge Base] KB[FAISS IndexFlatIP
768-dim cosine] DB[(SQLite
interactions · memories)] EMB[nomic-embed-text
via Ollama] end User --> CLI --> Chat --> Router Router --> P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 & P5 Router --> P6 & P7 & P8 & P9 & P10 Router --> KB KB --> DB KB --> EMB Router --> OBS[Observability
per-provider quality scores] ```

chunks.heading: Intent-Based Provider Routing Table
chunks.body: Chat (15s timeout): Groq → Antigravity → Claude → OpenAI → Hermes → Ollama. Summarization (20s): Antigravity → Groq → Claude → OpenAI → Ollama. Code (30s): Codex → Claude → Hermes → OpenAI → Groq → Ollama. Reasoning (45s): Hermes → Claude → OpenAI → Groq → Sakana → Antigravity → Ollama. Agentic (60s): Claude → OpenAI → Hermes → Groq → Antigravity → Ollama. Ollama is always the final local fallback for data-sensitive or offline scenarios.

chunks.heading: AI Gateway Governance Design
chunks.body: Production AI Gateway built on forge-router with: Tier 0 — async observability (non-blocking). Tier 1 — synchronous fast path under 2ms: rate limiter (token bucket per agent tier), circuit breaker (CLOSED/OPEN/HALF-OPEN state machine), token budget enforcer, prompt injection detector. Tier 2 — data classification: PII classifier routes confidential queries to Ollama (local), data sovereignty enforcement. Tier 3 — semantic cache (cosine similarity ≥ 0.95 returns cached response). Tier 4 — multi-agent competition. Tier 5 — DORA KPIs async (deploy frequency, lead time, MTTR, change failure rate). Tier 6 — immutable audit trail per request ID.
chunks.mermaid:
```mermaid graph TD subgraph KS[Enterprise Knowledge Sources] SKILL[SKILL.md · 11 Domains] CF[Confluence Runbooks] GL[GitLab ADRs] end subgraph GW[AI Gateway · Governance Layer] direction LR RL[Rate Limiter
Token Bucket / Agent] CB[Circuit Breaker
State Machine] PII[PII Classifier
Data Sovereignty] SC[Semantic Cache
Similarity ≥ 0.95] TBE[Token Budget Enforcer] PI[Prompt Injection Detector] end subgraph ROUTER[Multi-LLM Router] GRQ[Groq · Fast · Low-Cost] CLA[Claude · Deep Reasoning] GEM[Gemini Flash · Default] OLL[Ollama · Confidential Only] end KS --> GW GW --> ROUTER PII -- confidential --> OLL ROUTER --> OBS[Observability / DORA / Audit] ```

chunks.heading: AI-ForgeStream — Kubernetes-Native Media Processing
chunks.body: AI-ForgeStream is a Kubernetes-native automated media transcoding platform. A FastAPI control plane dynamically spawns ephemeral Kubernetes Job pods running FFmpeg for EBU R128 loudness normalization and HLS ABR transcoding. Output: 1080p/720p/480p HLS segments with master playlist, ITU-R BS.1770-4 compliant. Full Terraform IaC manages the Kubernetes namespace, PVC, RBAC, ServiceAccount, and ConfigMaps. Docker image: Alpine-based multi-role ffmpeg-forge:2.0. Verified telemetry: 81.9 seconds processing, -16.1 LUFS measured, target -24.0 LUFS.

chunks.heading: AI-ForgeStream Kubernetes Job Architecture
chunks.body: Client POSTs job to FastAPI control plane pod. Control plane creates a Kubernetes Job spec via Python SDK. Job pod mounts shared PVC at /data, reads source video, runs 2-pass FFmpeg loudness normalization (loudnorm filter, 48kHz stereo), transcodes to ABR ladder (1080p@4500kbps, 720p@2200kbps, 480p@800kbps), writes HLS m3u8 playlists and TS segments, writes metrics.json report, then terminates cleanly. Client polls GET /jobs/logs/{job_name} for streaming output.
chunks.mermaid:
```mermaid
graph TD
    Client([API Client]) -->|POST /jobs/submit| API[FastAPI Control Plane Pod]
    API -->|Create Job Spec| KubeAPI[Kubernetes Control Plane]
    KubeAPI -->|Schedule| Worker[FFmpeg Worker Job Pod]
    
    subgraph Storage
        PVC[(media-storage PVC)]
    end
    
    API -->|Mount /data| PVC
    Worker -->|Mount /data| PVC
    Worker -->|Read source| PVC
    Worker -->|Execute FFmpeg| Worker
    Worker -->|Write HLS + metrics| PVC
    Worker -->|Exit 0| KubeAPI
```

chunks.heading: EBS Auto-Resize — Event-Driven Disk Management with Slack Approval
chunks.body: Automated AWS EBS disk management pipeline with Slack-assisted human-in-the-loop confirmation. CloudWatch monitors disk space thresholds. When triggered, SNS sends to a centralized eu-west-1 Lambda hub (ExecuteAnalysis). Lambda analyzes storage inefficiencies across multiple XFS mount points (/var/logt/tr0/b00/t02, /var/logt/tr0/b01/t02), reports to Slack, and awaits operator EXPAND command. Slack slash command hits API Gateway → ExecuteResize Lambda. Resize Lambda calls Boto3 EBS modify_volume (25% safety cap), then SSM RunCommand to grow XFS filesystem (xfs_growfs). HMAC SHA256 signature verification prevents replay attacks.

chunks.heading: EBS Resize Event-Driven Architecture
chunks.body: Regional CloudWatch alarms per EC2 datanode → SNS → centralized API Gateway (eu-west-1) → ExecuteAnalysis Lambda → Slack report. Operator sends /expand → Slack Slash Command → API Gateway → ExecuteResize Lambda → Boto3 modify_volume → EBS → SSM RunCommand → xfs_growfs on EC2. Security: HMAC SHA256 request signing, 6-hour EBS modification cooldown, 25% maximum single-step growth cap, XFS-only filesystem support.
chunks.mermaid:
```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Regional
        A[EC2 Datanode] -- posts metrics --> B(CloudWatch Alarms)
        B -- threshold breach --> C(Amazon SNS)
    end
    subgraph Centralized [Centralized: eu-west-1]
        D{API Gateway}
        E[Lambda: ExecuteAnalysis]
        F[Lambda: ExecuteResize]
        G{API Gateway}
    end
    C --> D --> E
    E -- analysis report --> H[Slack]
    I[Operator] -- /expand --> H
    H -- slash command --> G --> F
    F -- modify_volume + xfs_growfs --> J[EBS Volumes]
```

chunks.heading: ai-router — Node.js Multi-LLM Router (v1)
chunks.body: ai-router is the Node.js predecessor to forge-router. Interactive chat CLI with persistent JSON-based session memory (chat-history.json, max 50 messages). Priority-ordered provider failover: Gemini CLI → Groq API → Ollama (local) → GitHub Copilot CLI → OpenAI API. Readline-based TUI with chalk coloring and ora spinners. Commands: /model, /history, /clear, /exit. This project demonstrates the evolution of multi-LLM routing concepts from Node.js to the more capable Python forge-router implementation.

chunks.heading: RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation
chunks.body: RAG is an architectural pattern that enhances LLM responses with retrieved context from an external knowledge base. Core pipeline: Chunking (fixed-size / semantic / recursive) → Embedding (numerical vector representation) → Vector Store → Retrieval (cosine/L2/dot product similarity) → Augmentation (prepend context to prompt) → Generation. Ingestion pipeline: Document → Preprocessing → Chunking → Embedding → Vector DB. Retrieval pipeline: Query → Embedding → Vector Search → Context Retrieval. AWS implementations: Bedrock Knowledge Bases (managed RAG), OpenSearch k-NN (vector store), EKS with LangChain workers. Production concerns: recall vs precision, re-ranking with cross-encoder, metadata filtering, avoiding 'lost in the middle' problem, preventing stale data.

chunks.heading: AI Agents — Architecture and Patterns
chunks.body: AI agents follow the ReAct loop: Thought → Action → Observation → repeat. Core components: Tool Use (function calling), Memory (in-context + external vector DB), Planning (task decomposition), Persona (behavioral role). Multi-agent systems use Orchestrator pattern: one planner agent decomposes tasks, multiple executor agents run in parallel, a critic agent evaluates results. AWS: Agents for Amazon Bedrock with Lambda as tool endpoints, EKS for LangGraph/CrewAI orchestration. Production pitfalls: infinite loops (max iterations guard), prompt injection in tool outputs, over-complexity, token cost per loop iteration.

chunks.heading: LLM Architecture and Production Deployment
chunks.body: Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder (T5, BART) vs decoder-only (GPT, Claude, Llama). Key parameters: tokens, context window, temperature (randomness), top-p (nucleus sampling). Fine-tuning vs RAG trade-offs: fine-tuning for style/domain adaptation, RAG for factual grounding with fresh data. AWS deployment options: Bedrock (serverless, managed), SageMaker (custom hosting and fine-tuning), EKS + vLLM/TGI + Karpenter (self-managed, GPU nodes). Production metrics: TTFT (time to first token), TPOT (time per output token), model quantization (FP16 → INT8/INT4 for memory efficiency).

chunks.heading: Kubernetes for AI Workloads
chunks.body: GPU scheduling with NVIDIA/AMD device plugins and time-slicing for shared GPU access. Karpenter for autoscaling G5/P4/Trn1 GPU instance types on AWS. Inference servers: vLLM (fast inference, continuous batching), TGI (Text Generation Inference), KServe (model serving standard). Kueue for job queuing and fair scheduling. KEDA for event-driven autoscaling on custom metrics like pending queue depth, token count, or LLM latency. AWS: NVIDIA Device Plugin, Neuron Device Plugin (Trainium/Inferentia), VPC Lattice for east-west traffic. Pitfalls: GPU cold starts (use Warm Pools), VRAM contention between models, scheduling delays for large jobs.

chunks.heading: MCP — Model Context Protocol
chunks.body: MCP is an open standard for connecting AI assistants to external data and tools. Core primitives: Servers (expose capabilities), Clients (consume them), Resources (data — files, DB, APIs), Tools (actions — code execution, API calls), Prompts (reusable templates). Transport: JSON-RPC over stdio (local, low-latency) or HTTP/SSE (remote, web-compatible). AI FORGE naturally aligns with MCP: skill wrappers → Tools, SKILL.md files → Resources, CLAUDE.md → Prompts, claude-skills.json → Tool Definitions. AWS: Lambda as MCP server (SSE transport), EKS microservices via VPC Lattice, IAM execution roles per MCP server.

chunks.heading: Vector Databases
chunks.body: Vector DBs store high-dimensional embeddings and enable fast approximate nearest-neighbour search. Indexing algorithms: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World — high recall, high memory), IVF (Inverted File Index — lower memory, batch indexing), Flat (exact search, small datasets). Similarity metrics: cosine (semantic similarity), L2/Euclidean (geometric distance), dot product. Metadata filtering narrows search space before vector scoring. Options: FAISS (in-process, CPU-only, no server), Qdrant (Rust, hybrid BM25+dense, self-hosted or cloud), ChromaDB (embedded Python, easy dev), Pinecone (managed cloud), pgvector (Postgres extension). AWS: OpenSearch k-NN, Aurora pgvector, EKS with Qdrant/Milvus/Weaviate via Helm.

chunks.heading: LangGraph — Stateful Agent Orchestration
chunks.body: LangGraph models agentic workflows as cyclic graphs: State (typed dict shared across nodes), Nodes (Python functions that read/write state), Edges (transitions — direct or conditional), Checkpoints (persist state to SQLite/Redis for resumability). Unlike LangChain chains (linear DAGs), LangGraph supports loops which are essential for ReAct agents that iterate until task completion. Conditional edges enable routing: if tool_call → execute_tool → back to reasoning; if done → output. AWS: individual nodes deployed as Lambda functions, persistent agents on EKS, DynamoDB as custom Checkpointer for state persistence.

chunks.heading: AI Security — Threats and Defences
chunks.body: Adversarial threats: Prompt Injection (direct — user overwrites system prompt; indirect — injected via tool output), Jailbreaking, Model Extraction, Training Data Poisoning, Evasion attacks. Data privacy concerns: PII/PHI/PCI leakage through LLM context, GDPR/CCPA compliance for EU/California data. Defense-in-Depth layers: Network (VPC PrivateLink, no public endpoints) → Identity (IAM/IRSA least-privilege) → Governance (NIST AI RMF, OWASP Top 10 LLMs) → Application (NeMo Guardrails, PII anonymisation before send) → Monitoring (LLM call auditing, anomaly detection). Patterns: Secure AI Gateway (central proxy with auth+audit), Privacy-Preserving RAG (retrieve then anonymise), Air-Gapped Inference on EKS for classified workloads.

chunks.heading: LLMOps — Production AI Operations
chunks.body: Prompt Management: version-controlled prompts, A/B testing, regression test suite per prompt version. Model Lifecycle: PEFT/LoRA for efficient fine-tuning, quantisation (INT4/INT8) for deployment. Evaluation: RAGAS (RAG-specific), G-Eval (general), LLM-as-a-Judge for scalable scoring. Guardrails: NeMo Guardrails (Colang policy language), AWS Bedrock Guardrails (managed content filtering). CI/CD for LLMs: automated evaluation gate before deployment, canary routing for new model versions, rollback on quality regression. AWS: Bedrock Guardrails, SageMaker Pipelines for training, EMR/Glue for data prep, EKS for evaluation workers.

chunks.heading: AI Observability
chunks.body: AI observability extends traditional o11y to LLM-specific signals. Trace dimensions: request ID, model, provider, TTFT, TPOT, total tokens, cost, prompt hash. Semantic monitoring: track topic drift and hallucination rate over time. Cost observability: token usage per user/team/workflow, budget alerting. Tools: LangSmith (LangChain native), Arize Phoenix (model monitoring), Langfuse (open-source LLM tracing), AWS X-Ray (distributed tracing), CloudWatch (custom namespaces for token metrics). Kubernetes: vLLM exposes Prometheus metrics endpoint for GPU utilisation, queue depth, generation throughput. Pitfalls: high-cardinality trace IDs, blind spots in multi-hop agentic workflows, metric overload.

chunks.heading: AI Platform Architecture Patterns
chunks.body: Router Pattern: dynamic model selection based on cost, latency, capability — matches user query complexity to model tier. Proxy Pattern: single centralised LLM endpoint with auth, rate limiting, cost tracking, provider failover — hides provider complexity from consumers. Orchestrator Pattern: coordinator agent breaks task into subtasks, delegates to specialist agents, aggregates results. Control Plane vs Data Plane separation: control plane manages routing rules and model registry; data plane handles inference traffic. Multi-Agent Systems: LangGraph for stateful loops, AutoGen for conversational agents, CrewAI for role-based teams. AWS: Bedrock (managed proxy), VPC + IAM for network/identity, EKS for self-managed inference. Pitfalls: single-provider lock-in, shadow AI (unmonitored usage), complexity overload.

chunks.heading: OpenAI API and Platform
chunks.body: GPT-4o (omni): multimodal — text, audio, image inputs and outputs in a single model. Chat Completions API: stateless, message array (system/user/assistant roles), streaming via SSE. Function Calling: structured JSON schema-defined tools, model decides when to invoke. Fine-tuning: JSONL training files, supervised on prompt-completion pairs, best for style adaptation. Embeddings: text-embedding-3-small (1536-dim, cost-efficient), text-embedding-3-large (3072-dim, highest accuracy). Assistants API: managed threads, file attachments, persistent state — but creates vendor lock-in. Production: TPM/RPM rate limits by tier, latency optimisation via streaming, privacy (disable data used for training for enterprise).

chunks.heading: Kubernetes Platform Engineering at Scale
chunks.body: Operating 19 EKS clusters across 7 global regions (India, EU-West, US-East, US-West, AP-Southeast, AP-Northeast, SA-East). Helm chart management with version-pinned dependencies. ArgoCD for GitOps continuous delivery — git commit triggers sync, automatic health checks, rollback on failure. Karpenter for node autoscaling (Spot + On-Demand, multiple instance families). OPA/Rego admission control policies. RBAC with least-privilege service accounts per namespace. Network policies (Calico). Istio service mesh for mTLS east-west traffic. EKS upgrade strategy: managed node group rolling update with PodDisruptionBudgets.

chunks.heading: Terraform and Infrastructure as Code
chunks.body: Module-based Terraform structure: network / compute / security / observability modules with clear interface boundaries. Remote state: S3 bucket with DynamoDB locking per environment (dev/staging/prod). Terragrunt for DRY configuration and dependency graph across modules. Provider and module version pinning for reproducibility. Drift detection via scheduled terraform plan in CI. Workspace strategy: separate state per region and environment. Import and refactoring: terraform import for adopting existing resources. State manipulation: terraform state mv, rm for safe restructuring. Sensitive outputs encrypted at rest.

chunks.heading: CI/CD Pipeline Architecture
chunks.body: Multi-stage pipeline architecture: lint → unit test → SAST (Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube) → Docker build → ECR push → integration test → ArgoCD GitOps deploy → smoke test → Slack notification. Branch protection: PR-required merge gates, status checks on all stages. Artifact strategy: immutable ECR tags using git SHA, no 'latest' in production. GitOps flow: merged commit triggers ArgoCD sync, ArgoCD applies Kubernetes manifests, health checks validate rollout. Rollback: git revert → pipeline → ArgoCD syncs previous manifest → zero downtime. Zero-downtime deployments: Blue/Green (switch service selector) or canary (Argo Rollouts, traffic weight shifting). Jenkins: scripted and declarative pipeline patterns, shared libraries.

chunks.heading: DevSecOps and Security Engineering
chunks.body: Shift-left security: SAST (SonarQube, Semgrep) and dependency scanning (Trivy, Snyk) in every PR. DAST in staging environment. Secrets management: AWS Secrets Manager + external-secrets operator for Kubernetes secret sync, no secrets in git. IAM least-privilege: roles scoped per service with no wildcard resource policies. Container image scanning before ECR push (Trivy, Grype). OPA/Rego policies for Kubernetes admission control (block privileged containers, enforce resource limits). Supply chain security: SBOM generation, Cosign image signing. Compliance automation: automated CIS benchmark checks, SOC2 evidence collection.

chunks.heading: Observability and SRE
chunks.body: Three pillars: metrics (Prometheus + Grafana), logs (Loki + Grafana), traces (Tempo / OpenTelemetry). SLO-based alerting: alert on burn rate against SLO budget, not raw thresholds. Dashboards as code: Grafana provisioning via ConfigMaps, version-controlled JSON. Distributed tracing: OpenTelemetry SDK instrumentation across all services, trace context propagation via W3C TraceContext. Dynatrace for full-stack APM: auto-discovery of services, OneAgent deployment on EKS nodes. Runbooks linked from alert annotations. Incident management: page → triage → mitigate → postmortem → action items. DORA metrics tracking: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, MTTR, change failure rate.

chunks.heading: AWS Cloud Architecture
chunks.body: Core services: EKS (managed Kubernetes), EC2, EBS, S3, RDS/Aurora, Lambda, API Gateway, CloudWatch, SNS, SQS, IAM, Secrets Manager, ECR, Route53, CloudFront. Advanced services: Bedrock (managed LLM), Karpenter (node autoscaling), VPC Lattice (service networking), PrivateLink (private connectivity), Macie (data classification), GuardDuty (threat detection). Multi-region architecture: active-active across 7 regions with Route53 latency-based routing and health checks. Cost optimisation: Spot instances with Karpenter, Reserved Instances for baseline, right-sizing with Compute Optimizer. Security: IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), VPC flow logs, Config Rules, Security Hub.

chunks.heading: Case Study: GitOps CI/CD Pipeline Reducing Deployment Lead Time by 70%
chunks.body: Challenge: A platform team managing 100+ microservices had inconsistent deployment practices. Some teams used manual kubectl apply, others had partial automation, and rollbacks took 45+ minutes. There was no visibility into deployment health and no standardised process across 7 regions. Solution: Designed and implemented a GitOps-first CI/CD platform using GitHub Actions, Docker, ECR, and ArgoCD across all microservices. Introduced branch protection with mandatory PR gates, multi-stage pipelines (lint → SAST → build → integration test → deploy), and immutable ECR image tagging via git SHA. ArgoCD was configured with ApplicationSets for multi-cluster deployment with automatic health check validation. Zero-downtime deployments via canary rollouts using Argo Rollouts with 10%→50%→100% traffic progression. Result: Deployment lead time reduced from 4 hours to 45 minutes (70% reduction). Rollback time dropped from 45 minutes to 3 minutes (git revert → pipeline → ArgoCD sync). Deployment frequency increased from 2x per week to 12x per week. Change failure rate dropped from 15% to 4%.

chunks.heading: Case Study: AI FORGE — Closing the Enterprise AI Execution Gap
chunks.body: Challenge: A platform engineering team managing 19 Kubernetes clusters across 7 global regions spent 60% of junior engineer time on context re-discovery — re-explaining platform architecture, credential flows, and regional quirks to AI assistants in every session. AI tools gave generic answers, not platform-specific ones. Credentials were being manually copy-pasted into prompts, creating security exposure. Solution: Built AI FORGE, an Enterprise AI Agent OS using the Crescent Architecture. 11 domain-specific skill packs (Python markdown files) encode expert knowledge per domain. A Global Brain CLAUDE.md always-loaded file gives the AI permanent platform context. A unified credentials wrapper injects auth at runtime so credentials never enter the AI context. A deny-list safety system blocks 23 destructive operations at the shell level before AI execution. Persistent cross-session memory retains behavioral corrections and incident context across sessions. Result: ~60% reduction in engineer troubleshooting ramp-up time. Zero credential leakage incidents since deployment. Junior engineers now perform senior-level platform operations with AI assistance. AI accuracy on platform-specific queries improved from ~40% (generic Claude) to ~90% (AI FORGE with domain context). 19 clusters across 7 regions managed consistently from a single AI interface.

chunks.heading: Case Study: Building forge-router — Zero-Downtime AI Infrastructure
chunks.body: Challenge: Teams using single LLM providers hit hard blockers: Groq rate limits during peak hours, Claude API quota exhaustion, OpenAI outages causing complete AI workflow failures. There was no resilience, no cost optimisation, and no intent-based routing — a reasoning-heavy query sent to a fast chat model gave poor results. Solution: Built forge-router, a production multi-LLM orchestration engine in Python. Intent classification (sub-1ms regex) routes queries to the optimal provider: Codex for code, Hermes for reasoning, Groq for chat (fastest), Ollama for sensitive data (local, no external send). Health-check-first routing skips unhealthy providers automatically. FAISS + SQLite embedded RAG stores interaction history and retrieves relevant context for each prompt (nomic-embed-text embeddings, 768-dim, cosine similarity ≥ 0.55). Per-provider quality scoring builds an observability layer that identifies which providers give best results per intent type. Result: Zero full AI outages since deployment (previous: 2-3 per week). 40% cost reduction by routing routine queries to free-tier Groq instead of paid Claude. Response quality improved by matching query type to optimal model. Session memory persists across provider switches, maintaining conversation context through failovers.

chunks.heading: Case Study: EBS Auto-Resize — Eliminating 2am Disk Emergency Pages
chunks.body: Challenge: EC2 datanodes running XFS volumes repeatedly hit disk capacity limits at unpredictable times, causing 2am on-call pages. The manual remediation process — SSH → df -h → manual EBS modify_volume via console → xfs_growfs → verify → document — took 45 minutes per incident and required senior engineers with AWS console access. Disk space growth patterns were also not being analysed, only reacted to. Solution: Built a fully automated event-driven EBS disk management pipeline. CloudWatch alarms per datanode monitor XFS mount points. On threshold breach: SNS → API Gateway → ExecuteAnalysis Lambda — which analyses actual disk usage patterns (not just free space), calculates optimal growth amount (max 25% cap for safety), and posts a detailed Slack report with the full analysis. Operator reviews and sends /expand slash command. Slack → API Gateway → ExecuteResize Lambda → Boto3 modify_volume → SSM RunCommand → xfs_growfs on the live EC2 without reboot. HMAC SHA256 signature verification on every Slack command. 6-hour cooldown period enforced between EBS modifications. Result: On-call escalations for disk issues reduced from ~8/month to 0. Average remediation time dropped from 45 minutes to 4 minutes (analysis automatic, operator just approves). Zero accidental over-provisioning incidents due to safety cap and human-in-the-loop approval. Full audit trail of every resize event.

chunks.heading: Case Study: DevSecOps — Shifting Security Left Across 100+ Pipelines
chunks.body: Challenge: Security reviews were a bottleneck — PRs sat for days waiting for manual security sign-off. Container images were deployed with known CVEs because scanning happened post-deployment. Secrets occasionally appeared in git history. IAM policies used wildcard resources for convenience. Solution: Implemented shift-left DevSecOps across all 100+ pipelines. SAST with SonarQube and Semgrep integrated into PR checks — PRs blocked on high-severity findings. Trivy scanning before every ECR push — image rejected if critical CVEs found. External-secrets operator syncs AWS Secrets Manager values into Kubernetes secrets at runtime, eliminating all hardcoded secrets. Git history scanning (git-secrets, TruffleHog) in pre-commit hooks. OPA/Rego admission controller policies: block privileged containers, enforce resource limits, require non-root user, ban 'latest' image tag. IAM roles scoped per service account (IRSA), all wildcard resource ARNs replaced with specific resource patterns. Result: Zero high-severity CVEs reaching production (previously: ~3/month). Security review bottleneck eliminated — automated gates replace manual review for standard checks. Secret exposure incidents: 0 in 18 months. IAM permission scope reduced by ~65% across all service accounts.

chunks.heading: Case Study: Observability Platform — From Alert Noise to SLO-Based Operations
chunks.body: Challenge: The monitoring setup was generating 200+ alerts per day, most of which were false positives or not actionable. Engineers had alert fatigue — critical pages were being ignored because of noise. There was no distributed tracing, so debugging a slow request required manually correlating logs across 5+ services. MTTR was averaging 4.5 hours. Solution: Rebuilt observability on Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + Tempo + OpenTelemetry. Defined SLOs for every critical service (availability, latency p99). Converted all alerts from threshold-based to burn-rate-based — only alert when SLO budget is burning at an unsustainable rate. OpenTelemetry SDK instrumented across all services with automatic context propagation. Grafana dashboards as code via ConfigMaps. Runbooks linked directly from alert annotations. Dynatrace OneAgent deployed on all EKS nodes for full-stack APM and automatic service discovery. For AI workloads: Langfuse integrated for LLM call tracing, token usage, and quality scoring. Result: Alert volume reduced from 200+/day to 12/day, all actionable. MTTR reduced from 4.5 hours to 38 minutes. Debug sessions that previously took hours now take minutes with distributed traces. 99.99% uptime SLA maintained for 18 consecutive months.

chunks.heading: forge-SRE — Production Observability & SRE Governance Platform
chunks.body: forge-SRE is a full SRE governance and observability platform built across a 19-cluster Kubernetes estate in 7 AWS regions. A structured audit discovered a 21/24 deviation score across 6 governance domains: fragmented monitoring tooling, zero distributed tracing, no SLO framework, AI workloads running completely blind (0% telemetry), DORA metrics manually estimated monthly, and 200+ threshold alerts per day with 87% false-positive rate. Built a centralised observability stack: OpenTelemetry Collector as a DaemonSet on every EKS node collecting metrics (OTLP gRPC/HTTP, Prometheus scrape), logs (filelog from /var/log/pods), and traces. Processors: memory_limiter 400MB, batch, resource_detection (EKS+EC2 auto-tagging), tail_sampling (100% errors, 5% success). Exporters to Prometheus (30d), Loki on S3 (31d), Tempo on S3 (7d). Grafana as single pane with SLO burn-rate dashboards, DORA metrics (live via ArgoCD webhooks), and AI workload dashboards (TTFT, tokens, cost). SLO burn-rate alerting: fast-burn (1h, 14x rate) to PagerDuty, slow-burn (6h, 6x rate) to Slack. All Terraform + Helm IaC.

chunks.heading: Case Study: forge-SRE — SRE Governance Audit from 21/24 Deviation to Production Observability
chunks.body: Challenge: Three P1 incidents in one quarter exposed a systemic observability failure across 19 EKS clusters. Incident A: a latency regression went undetected 6 hours because a CloudWatch alarm had been silenced 4 months prior. Incident B: a P1 outage took 4.5 hours to resolve — 2.5 hours were spent correlating logs across 6 tools rather than fixing the issue. Incident C: an LLM inference service produced degraded responses (hallucinations, slow TTFT) for 3 days before anyone noticed — there was zero AI workload instrumentation. Formal governance audit across 6 domains scored 21/24 deviation (critical or non-compliant in every dimension). 200-250 alerts/day, 87% false positives. Engineers routinely muted alert channels. Solution: 6-week phased remediation: OTel Collector DaemonSet deployed to all 19 clusters, unified metric namespace via OTel SDK push (replacing 4 fragmented tools), structured JSON logs via Loki, distributed tracing via Tempo with W3C TraceContext, SLO burn-rate rules (23 SLOs defined for all critical services), AI workload instrumentation (TTFT, tokens, cost, quality per LLM call), DORA metrics automated via ArgoCD webhooks and GitHub Actions events. Stakeholder sign-off at each phase gate. Governance framework established as permanent standard — new services must define SLOs before production, all alerts must be burn-rate based. Result: Alert volume 200+/day → 12/day, all actionable. MTTR 4.5 hours → 38 minutes. Root-cause identification for latency: 2-4 hours → 5-15 minutes. AI workload visibility: 0% → 100% per-call telemetry. DORA metrics: manual monthly → real-time. Uptime SLA: 99.99% for 18 consecutive months. ~100 engineering hours saved per month in toil reduction.

chunks.heading: AI-ForgeStream — Kubernetes-Native Media Processing Pipeline
chunks.body: AI-ForgeStream replaces long-lived, always-on EC2 media transcoding instances with ephemeral Kubernetes Jobs spawned on demand by a FastAPI control plane. Pay only for compute during actual job execution — zero idle cost. Architecture: API client POSTs to FastAPI control plane → creates K8s Job spec via Python SDK → Kubernetes schedules an ephemeral FFmpeg worker pod → pod reads source video from shared PVC (50Gi ReadWriteMany), runs 2-pass loudness normalisation + ABR ladder encoding, writes output (HLS segments + master.m3u8 + metrics.json) back to PVC, then terminates cleanly (Exit 0). 2-pass FFmpeg pipeline: Pass 1 measures integrated loudness (LUFS), true peak (dBTP), loudness range (LRA). Pass 2 applies correction targeting -24.0 LUFS (EBU R128 broadcast standard), -2.0 dBTP max, 48kHz stereo AAC. ABR ladder: 1080p@4500kbps H.264 High, 720p@2200kbps H.264 Main, 480p@800kbps H.264 Baseline. HLS master playlist referencing all three. Kubernetes RBAC scoped to Job create/get/list/delete only. All infrastructure Terraform IaC.

chunks.heading: Case Study: AI-ForgeStream — Eliminating Idle Media Infrastructure with Kubernetes Jobs
chunks.body: Challenge: Media transcoding workflows ran on long-lived EC2 instances that were idle 90% of the time but paid for 24/7. Jobs were triggered manually via SSH with no queueing, no parallelism, no standardised output formats, and no programmatic API. Audio loudness levels were inconsistent across outputs — variable LUFS levels caused quality issues downstream for broadcast distribution. There was no observability: no job metrics, no completion telemetry, no SLA on processing time. Solution: Built AI-ForgeStream, a Kubernetes-native media processing platform. FastAPI control plane exposes REST API (POST /jobs/submit). On job submission, FastAPI calls Kubernetes Python SDK to create an ephemeral Job spec pointing to ffmpeg-forge:2.0 Docker image (Alpine multi-stage, ~120MB). K8s schedules a worker pod on available node. Worker reads source from shared 50Gi ReadWriteMany PVC, runs 2-pass FFmpeg: Pass 1 measures actual loudness profile (integrated LUFS, true peak dBTP, LRA). Pass 2 applies measured corrections targeting EBU R128 broadcast standard (-24.0 LUFS, -2.0 dBTP, 48kHz AAC stereo). Then encodes 3-tier ABR ladder to HLS (1080p/720p/480p). Writes HLS segments, master.m3u8, and structured metrics.json to PVC. Pod exits 0 and terminates — no idle cost. Clients poll GET /jobs/logs/{name} for streaming pod log output. RBAC: control plane ServiceAccount scoped to Job create/get/list/delete only — no cluster-admin. Result: Idle compute cost: 100% always-on EC2 → 0% (pods terminate on completion). Loudness: variable manual → ITU-R BS.1770-4 compliant on every output. Job trigger: SSH manual → REST API. Parallel capacity: 1 EC2 → Kubernetes horizontal scheduler (unlimited parallel jobs). Processing time verified: 81.9 seconds for a sample transcode. Full telemetry per job in metrics.json.

chunks.heading: Full Technology Stack
chunks.body: Cloud: AWS (primary — EKS, Bedrock, Lambda, RDS, ECR, S3, IAM, CloudWatch). Container Orchestration: Kubernetes (EKS), Helm, ArgoCD, Karpenter, Kueue, KEDA. Infrastructure as Code: Terraform, Terragrunt, Ansible. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, ArgoCD, Argo Rollouts. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Tempo, OpenTelemetry, Dynatrace, Langfuse. Security: Vault/OpenBao, AWS Secrets Manager, Trivy, OPA/Rego, Semgrep, SonarQube. Databases: MySQL/RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, SQLite. AI/ML: AWS Bedrock, Claude, Groq, Gemini, OpenAI, Ollama, FAISS, Qdrant, ChromaDB, LangChain, LangGraph. Languages: Python (primary), TypeScript/Node.js, Bash. Messaging: SNS, SQS, Kafka. CDN/Networking: CloudFront, Route53, VPC, ALB.

chunks.heading: Production RAG + AI Gateway Architecture Diagram
chunks.body: Full enterprise RAG pipeline from knowledge sources through ingestion, vector storage, hybrid search, AI gateway governance, multi-LLM routing, observability, and continuous improvement loop.

```mermaid

```

chunks.heading: Custom Terraform Provider Development (Plugin Framework)
chunks.body: As Technical Lead at Thales Group, Vikash builds custom Terraform Providers using the HashiCorp Terraform Plugin Framework in Go, extending infrastructure automation to proprietary internal APIs that off-the-shelf providers (AWS/Azure/GCP) don't cover. A hands-on reference project (terraform-plugin-framework-lab) demonstrates the pattern end-to-end: a thales_keystore resource backed by a REST mock API server, exercising the full provider lifecycle — Metadata/Schema/Configure at the provider level, Create/Read/Update/Delete/ImportState per resource — communicating with Terraform Core over the Terraform Provider Protocol (gRPC), with the provider installed via Terraform's local filesystem mirror during development. Create and Read are fully wired end-to-end against the mock API; Update/Delete, ImportState, plan modifiers, module design, and acceptance testing are in active development. Also driving integration of GenAI-powered tooling into infrastructure automation workflows to reduce manual operational effort. Tech stack: Terraform, Go, Terraform Plugin Framework, Kubernetes.

chunks.heading: forge-router Preview Window Lifecycle
chunks.body: Two-tier preview lifecycle: HTTP server bound once on :7654 for the full session; WKWebView window toggled independently via Ctrl+P or /p. Preview polls GET /state every 800ms for content updates. On open, last response is immediately restored — no blank window. Auto-open fires when response contains mermaid/images. Only visual content (mermaid, images, media) clears the preview window — plain text responses leave last state visible. Key binding: Ctrl+P sets _ctrl_p_pending flag and exits prompt via KeyboardInterrupt — no I/O inside key binding to avoid corrupting prompt_toolkit display.
chunks.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Terminal (forge chat)
    participant S as HTTP Server :7654
    participant W as WKWebView Window
    T->>S: _ensure_server() — bind once per session
    T->>W: subprocess Popen (preview_window.py)
    W->>S: GET / (load HTML shell)
    loop every 800ms
        W->>S: GET /state (JSON)
        S-->>W: content + provider + model + msg_count
        W->>W: render markdown + mermaid + hljs
    end
    T->>S: preview.write(new_content)
    T->>W: terminate() on toggle OFF
    T->>S: server.shutdown() on forge exit

chunks.heading: forge-router RAG Knowledge Base
chunks.body: FAISS IndexFlatIP with 768-dimensional cosine similarity using nomic-embed-text embeddings via Ollama. SQLite stores raw interactions and consolidated memory facts. Auto-consolidation: every 10 interactions, Groq extracts key facts from the batch and stores them as high-quality memory chunks. Retrieval threshold: cosine similarity >= 0.55. Top-4 memories injected as context block into every prompt. Cold-start safe: retrieval skipped when index is empty. Graceful degradation: FAISS wrapped in try/except with _FAISS_OK flag — app starts cleanly even if faiss-cpu not installed.
chunks.mermaid:
graph LR
    Prompt --> Embed[nomic-embed-text 768-dim]
    Embed --> FAISS[FAISS IndexFlatIP cosine]
    FAISS --> Top4[Top-4 memories threshold 0.55]
    Top4 --> CtxBlock[Context block injected into prompt]
    CtxBlock --> LLM[LLM Provider]
    LLM --> Response
    Response --> Score[Observability quality score]
    Score --> SQLite[(SQLite interactions)]
    SQLite --> Consolidate[Auto-consolidate every 10]
    Consolidate --> Facts[Extract facts via Groq]
    Facts --> Embed

chunks.heading: Case Study: Terraform — Multi-Region IaC Across 7 Regions
chunks.body: Challenge: Infrastructure across 7 regions was partially Terraform, partially ClickOps, partially tribal knowledge. State files local on engineers' laptops. No locking — two engineers running terraform apply simultaneously caused state corruption. Module versions not pinned. Drift invisible and growing. Solution: Full Terraform platform — remote state (S3 + DynamoDB locking), module versioning, Terragrunt DRY wrappers, drift detection CI job (daily terraform plan diffed against expected-empty). Provider pinned at ~> 5.0. All ClickOps resources imported. Result: State corruption incidents 2/quarter → 0. Drift detection automated daily. 2 of 7 regions under IaC → 7 of 7. ClickOps percentage ~40% → <5%.

chunks.heading: Career Journey — From Broadcast Engineering to Cloud/AI Platform Leadership
chunks.body: Vikash's 16+ year career spans six employers and a distinctive pivot from broadcast/media engineering into cloud, DevOps, and AI platform leadership. TVS-E Service Tec (Aug-Dec 2009): chip-level hardware diagnostics on Nokia mobile prototypes. Essel Shyam Communication (Apr 2010-2012): started as a broadcast trainee on UTV channels (Bindass, Action, World Movies), then broadcast engineer for Star India's playout/MCR — Harris automation (D-Series, 26 channels), Viz-Artist graphics engine, HD/Dolby systems, Sony VTR, Vikinx video routing. Tata Sky Ltd / Tata Sky Binge OTT (2012-2019): governed live ingestion and transmission for 450+ TV channels, later upgraded to 800+ channels via an Irdeto CAS/baseband compression upgrade; built serverless AWS Lambda + Step Functions pipelines for stream transcoding and HLS/DASH packaging; VoD content management over Dalet MAM with subtitling and metadata workflows. Hughes Communications India (2019-2022): satellite communication hub design (HPA/DTR/AUPC/ACU configuration), Link Budget and EIRP calculations, HTS/LEO satellite operations, and datacenter-to-cloud migration planning for banking, maritime, and aviation clients. GlobalXperts Inc (2022-2023): owned production operations for NHL hockey and cricket live streaming on AWS MediaLive/MediaConnect/CloudFront under strict SLAs. Devo Technology (2023-2026): started as a SaaS Production Engineer, then SaaS Production Cloud Engineer, based in Madrid, Spain (Apr 2023-Feb 2026) managing SIEM cloud infrastructure and SOAR platform integration with SSO for Devo's own security-analytics product; promoted to Lead Platform Engineer in Noida, India (Feb 2026-present) — 19 Kubernetes clusters across 7 regions, built AI FORGE. Thales Group (2026-present): Technical Lead, custom Terraform Providers and GenAI automation tooling. The throughline: broadcast/RF engineering discipline (strict uptime SLAs, hardware-level troubleshooting) carried directly into cloud reliability engineering and now AI platform engineering.

chunks.heading: Education and Certifications
chunks.body: Education: MBA in Information Technology & Project Management from Sikkim Manipal University (Distance Education, 2012); B.Tech in Electronics & Communication Engineering from UPTU Lucknow (2009). Certifications: AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Associate (2023-2026), AWS Certified DevOps Engineer, AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner, Certified Linux Engineer (LFCE), ITIL Foundation Certification, ServiceNow Fundamentals, Java-Core, Claude 101 – AI Operational Certification (Anthropic), AWS Media Services – Elemental.

chunks.heading: Current Learning Focus (2026)
chunks.body: Active learning investments for 2026, prioritized by depth and impact: AI Gateway Design and RAG Architectures (deepest investment), LLMOps and Multi-Agent Systems, MCP (Model Context Protocol) and LangGraph for stateful agent orchestration, AI Governance, Langfuse for LLM observability, and Qdrant vector database. Structured as a self-directed 12-day AI Platform Engineer / GenAI Solutions Architect crash course (github.com/vikas0486/AI-Learning), accelerating the transition from a Senior DevOps/Infrastructure background into AI Platform and GenAI Architect roles. Approach is hands-on: building reference implementations — the custom Terraform Provider work at Thales, forge-router's RAG pipeline — rather than studying theory alone.

chunks.heading: 90-Day DevOps/Platform Leadership Plan
chunks.body: Vikash's first-90-days approach to a new DevOps/Platform leadership role follows a Discover, Stabilize, Accelerate cadence. Days 1-30: stakeholder 1:1s across Engineering/Architecture/Security/Product, shadowing on-call rotations, auditing existing CI/CD and IaC coverage, and establishing a DORA metrics baseline (deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, MTTR) rather than assuming problems. Days 30-60: quick wins — fix the highest-impact observability gap, standardize one CI/CD pipeline as a reference implementation, stand up a shared engineering metrics dashboard visible to all teams (not just leadership), and resolve the single biggest deployment reliability pain point. Days 60-90: a 6-month modernization roadmap with executive alignment, a deployment governance framework, DevSecOps integration kickoff, and identification of internal DevOps champions. Ongoing: monthly DORA metric reviews and quarterly maturity re-assessment. Core philosophy: move fast, but move with data — pivot immediately if a critical production issue surfaces in week two rather than sticking to a rigid plan.

chunks.heading: DevOps Maturity Assessment Framework
chunks.body: Vikash evaluates an organization's DevOps maturity using a DORA-aligned, five-dimension framework rather than starting with a tooling audit: (1) Deployment Pipeline Health — CI/CD automation coverage, deployment frequency, manual-vs-automated gate ratio; assessed first because deployment frequency and MTTR data never lies. (2) Observability & Incident Response — telemetry coverage, alert signal-to-noise ratio, MTTR baseline, runbook automation; assessed second. (3) Infrastructure Practice — IaC adoption rate, drift detection, environment consistency. (4) Security Integration (DevSecOps) — secrets management maturity, pipeline security controls, compliance-as-code. (5) Cultural & Governance Maturity — PR review cycle time, change failure rate, on-call fairness. A common maturity-signal check: teams with Kubernetes everywhere that still SSH into boxes to make changes reveal a governance gap that tooling alone won't fix.

chunks.heading: Software Delivery Performance Metrics Framework
chunks.body: Vikash anchors delivery measurement in the DORA four key metrics — deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and MTTR — then layers in three additional tiers: pipeline health (build success rate, build duration trend, queue/wait time), engineering effectiveness (PR review cycle time, review coverage, test coverage trend, technical debt index), and operational health (infrastructure drift rate, alert noise ratio, on-call incident volume, cost per deployment). At Devo Technology this fed Grafana dashboards built on Prometheus with GitOps-managed alert configuration — thresholds and routing controlled entirely via YAML in Git, giving a full audit trail of who changed what. For executive reporting, raw percentages are translated into business language — not '94% pipeline success rate' but '2 of 50 deployments last week required rollback, impacting 3 customer-facing services for 18 minutes' — because that framing is what actually drives leadership action.

chunks.heading: PR Review and Release Governance Model
chunks.body: Vikash's model splits release governance into three layers so human reviewers spend attention on judgment, not mechanical checks. Automated gate layer (DevOps-owned): secret scanning, linting, SAST, dependency/SCA scanning, container image scanning, Terraform plan validation, and test coverage thresholds — all block the PR automatically before a human ever opens it. Human review layer (Engineering-owned): architecture and design review, business logic correctness, performance impact, API contract review. Release governance layer (DevOps + Engineering): environment promotion policy, change-window compliance, rollback plan validation, blue-green/canary traffic-shift approval. At Devo Technology, every configuration change — alert thresholds, routing policies, deployment parameters — went through a Git PR with CI validation as the governance mechanism itself, rather than a separate approval tool, making unauthorized configuration drift operationally impossible.

chunks.heading: Case Study: Custom Terraform Provider for On-Premises Banking Infrastructure (CIBC)
chunks.body: Background: While at Devo Technology, Vikash worked on infrastructure for CIBC's European on-premises operations — regulatory data-residency requirements meant no AWS/Azure/GCP was usable at all. Every infrastructure change (VM provisioning, database instances, HSM/keystore management, network segmentation) was executed manually against internal systems, ticket-driven with no declarative source of truth and no drift detection. What's being built (in progress, not yet delivered to CIBC): a custom Terraform Provider in Go using the Terraform Plugin Framework, whose client talks to an internal on-prem infrastructure control plane over a purpose-built gRPC/protobuf schema instead of a public-cloud REST API — the same pattern being developed hands-on in the terraform-plugin-framework-lab reference project. Two gRPC layers are distinct: Terraform Core's standard Provider Protocol (gRPC, protocol-agnostic) versus the provider's own client-to-control-plane gRPC layer, which is the part no off-the-shelf provider can do. Example resources: onprem_database_instance, onprem_keystore. Projected impact (design target, not yet measured): infrastructure change lead time from hours to minutes, drift visibility from none to direct terraform plan output, audit trail from ticket history to full state/plan history in version control.

chunks.heading: Devo Technology — SIEM/SOAR Production Engineering in Madrid (2023-2026)
chunks.body: Before becoming Lead Platform Engineer, Vikash's first roughly two years and eleven months at Devo Technology (Apr 2023 - Feb 2026) were spent based in Madrid, Spain as a SaaS Production Engineer, then SaaS Production Cloud Engineer — managing SIEM cloud infrastructure on AWS and integrating a SOAR platform with SIEM via SSO for Devo's own cloud-native security-analytics product. This production-support role is the direct context for hands-on incident work on customer SIEM deployments, including resolving JVM OutOfMemoryError incidents on a mission-critical CaixaBank cluster. In February 2026 he transitioned to Lead Platform Engineer in Noida, India, taking on platform engineering, infrastructure automation, and GenAI-powered operational tooling (AI FORGE) across the full Devo estate — 19 Kubernetes clusters across 7 global regions.
